opponent_lineup.source: No 2026 batting order crawled yet, but the 2025 book on the Brewers is thick: three meetings, and we took the series 2-1 — an opening 2-14 loss, then a 9-8 nailbiter and a 17-11 win. They bat right-handed top to bottom (14 of 15 hitters on file are righties), so expect a pull-heavy, left-side attack — shade the left-center gap and dare them to go oppo. The lone lefty, Jed Donahue (#12), is the exception who pulls to the RIGHT, so slide the defense over when he steps in. Full at-bat-by-at-bat breakdown and the per-hitter alignment calls are on the Brewers Scout page.

rules: ROAD GAME at Boy Scout Park, 12 Cahoon Rd, Boxford — first pitch 6:30, be there by 5:30. It's the Brewers, who we beat twice in three tries in 2025 after dropping the opener. Flinny Tailgate is on.

Twelve hands on deck — Matt Watson just confirmed, and his glove changes the picture at third. OUT: Andrew Amann (#21), Thacher Knight (#20), Corey Cheever, Jon Sorenson, and Luke McArdle. With Amann out the outfield still reshuffles — Perry DiLibero to shortstop, Dennis Marks to left field, John Flinn to left-center — and the catching splits three ways: Bruno four innings, Ryan two, Konen one. The order is ranked off the 2025 body of work.

WATSON AT THE HOT CORNER: our best third baseman is back and plays all seven at 3B — which frees Eric Klatt to be a pure arm (he opens on the mound, innings 1–5) and frees Ed Fitzpatrick, our second-best third baseman, to shift to second base. Joe McLean closes on the mound (innings 6–7), back in the fold for the first time this year.

Twelve players, ten spots — so two fielders rest each inning. Five go wire to wire: DiLibero (short), Flinn (center), Marks (left), Sang (first), and Watson (third) — their spots have no backup with so many bats out. The other seven each take exactly two defensive breathers; nobody sits three times. Everyone hits all game; SIT means a defensive rest only. The grid shows where you are each inning.

grid.why: A .565 on-base with exactly one strikeout in all of 2025 — the man reaches base like it's a legal obligation and refuses to ever hand the at-bat back. Leadoff is just the paperwork catching up to the talent. Rests the first two defensively, then anchors the rest — two innings behind the plate, the others in right-center.

A .644 average with a .631 on-base isn't a stat line, it's a parking violation nobody made him pay. He missed time and the lineup still saved his 2-hole seat. With Amann out he's the best outfielder we've got, so he patrols left-center all seven.

Hit .524 with zero walks and zero strikeouts in 63 trips — he has never once considered taking a pitch, and the box score keeps agreeing with him. Bats third to set the table; he handles four innings behind the plate and slides to right-center for one, where his glove plays best.

With Amann off tonight, the franchise bat is yours, Perry: 14 homers and 53 RBI in 2025, both team highs. Cleanup isn't so much a lineup decision as a weather forecast. Mans shortstop all seven — the bat AND the glove carry a load.

.514 at the plate and zero errors in the field in 2025 — the rare man who's calm in the box and calmer at first base. Bats fifth to keep the line moving and mans first all seven, where very little gets by him.

.476 with seven doubles of pure gap larceny, batting sixth. With Watson back at third, Klatt's a pure arm tonight: he opens on the mound and goes five strong, then hands it to Joe and takes his rest. We feed him, we thank him.

.473 with four triples — at this level a triple is two miracles in one swing: crush it AND agree to run the whole way. Our second-best third baseman, but with Watson back to own the hot corner he shifts to second base for five innings — same sure hands, new dirt.

The man we were missing. A perfect 1.000 fielding percentage at the hot corner and a quiet .469 in 2025 — and his arrival lets the whole infield exhale: Watson takes third base all seven, so Klatt goes back to being just an arm and Ed slides to second. Bats eighth tonight, but his glove plays top-of-the-order.

Never mind the .394 — the .652 slugging with three bombs and eight doubles is the real headline. Boom-or-bust pop batting ninth to flip the order over with a bang. Tonight he trades the dirt for grass and patrols left field all seven.

Back in the fold for the first time this year: .368 with a sneaky .429 on-base, batting tenth. Roams right and spells second early, grabs a couple breathers, then closes the game on the mound across the last two innings. A full evening's labor.

Eight plate trips and a .250 on file — basically a rumor with batting gloves. The 11-hole is the audition booth: he patrols right field most of the night with an inning at second when Joe takes the mound. Square one up and the spreadsheet finally learns to spell Doherty.

The .208 keeps him in the cellar, but a team-high six walks says he treats a free base as a personal mission — and in this league, taking ball four is basically civil disobedience. Bats twelfth, patrols right-center, takes a turn in right, and straps on the gear behind the plate to close.
